It feels like everyone’s got a workshop full of sawdust they’re trying to sell you, but most of it is just junk wood. Dealing with all this fake content on social media, for me, it boils down to being a meticulous inspector, just like when I’m framing a new deck. You can’t just slap it together and call it done; you have to check every joint. The first thing I do is stop and look at who is saying the thing. Is it some slick-looking page with no real history, or is it someone I actually know? If it’s a page, I check their profile like I check the structural integrity of a beam—are they consistent? Do they have a history of sharing things that are clearly blown out of proportion? Because sometimes, the most outrageous claims are made by the people who seem the loudest, which is usually just noise. Since I’m naturally curious, I get the urge to dig deeper, but I have to keep that curiosity pointed toward facts, not just sensationalism. I’ve learned that if a headline makes my blood pressure jump right away, that’s usually a sign to slow down. It’s engineered to get that immediate reaction, that emotional kick, so you share it without thinking. I treat it like faulty lumber; you don't use it because you think it looks good. Instead, I try to trace the information back to a reliable source, something concrete, like a government report or a major news outlet I trust, even if it’s a bit dry. I’m not looking for the flashiest story; I’m looking for the solid build. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: When you spend as much time online as I do, you start to see all sorts of nonsense floating around, and frankly, it’s a headache. It feels like everyone’s got a workshop full of sawdust they’re trying to sell you, but most of it is just junk wood. Dealing with all this fake content on social media, for me, it boils down to being a meticulous inspector, just like when I’m framing a new deck. You can’t just slap it together and call it done; you have to check every joint. The first thing I do is stop and look at who is saying the thing. Is it some slick-looking page with no real history, or is it someone I actually know? If it’s a page, I check their profile like I check the structural integrity of a beam—are they consistent? Do they have a history of sharing things that are clearly blown out of proportion? Because sometimes, the most outrageous claims are made by the people who seem the loudest, which is usually just noise. Since I’m naturally curious, I get the urge to dig deeper, but I have to keep that curiosity pointed toward facts, not just sensationalism. I’ve learned that if a headline makes my blood pressure jump right away, that’s usually a sign to slow down. It’s engineered to get that immediate reaction, that emotional kick, so you share it without thinking. I treat it like faulty lumber; you don't use it because you think it looks good. Instead, I try to trace the information back to a reliable source, something concrete, like a government report or a major news outlet I trust, even if it’s a bit dry. I’m not looking for the flashiest story; I’m looking for the solid build. It takes discipline, a real steady hand, to sift through the clutter and keep only the material that’s sound.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a highly extraverted, conscientious, and low-agreeable carpenter who spends many hours online. The tone must be direct, practical, and slightly skeptical, mirroring a skilled tradesperson's pragmatic approach. The advice must be framed using analogies related to carpentry or building (inspection, structural integrity, solid material). The discussion must focus on the practical steps of vetting information rather than abstract theory.
